coredata.dc:title: Photostability and phototoxicity of graphene quantum dots interacting with red blood cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Kulahava, Tatsiana
Belko, Nikita
Parkhats, Marina
Bahdanava, Anastasiya
Lepeshkevich, Sergei
Chizhevsky, Vyacheslav
Mogilevtsev, Dmitri
coredata.dc:description: Here we discuss fluorescent properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) interacting with the membranes of red blood cells. We report the results of spectroscopic, microscopic, and photon-counting measurements of the GQDs in different surroundings for uncovering specific features of the GQD fluorescence, and describe two observed phenomena important for implementation of the GQDs as fluorescent labels and agents for drug delivery. Firstly, the GQDs can suffer from photodegradation but also can be stabilized in the presence of antioxidants (reduced glutathione, N-acetylcysteine, or 1,4-hydroquinone). Secondly, GQDs can accumulate in red blood cell membranes without compromising the viability of the cells but also can induce hemolysis in the presence of visible light. We discuss mechanisms and regimes of the photodegradation, stabilization, interaction of the GQDs with red blood cell membranes, and hemolysis. Notably, photohemolysis for the case is dependent on the light dose and GQD concentration but not caused by the production of reactive oxygen species.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Red blood cells
Cell membranes
Hemolysis
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Localization of the Graphene Quantum Dots in Red Blood Cells
Spectral Properties of the Graphene Quantum Dots in Red Blood Cells
Light-Induced Effects in Red Blood Cells Treated with the Graphene Quantum Dots
Photodegradation of the Graphene Quantum Dots and Photon-Counting Measurements

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Belarusian Republican Foundation for Fundamental Research (project no. M22MB-019 and F22B-008 ) and by the National Academy of Sciences of Belarus through the Program “Photonics and Electronics for Innovation 2021–2025” (Project 1.8). We thank our colleague Dr. Lena Golubewa for a fruitful discussion and valuable comments and the Center for Analytical and Spectral Measurements of the B.I. Stepanov Institute of Physics for fluorescence measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical bright-field and fluorescence images of untreated RBCs (control sample) and RBCs treated with 1 or 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs for 1 h. Note that for the control sample, the fluorescent background is brighter than the cells, in contrast to the RBCs treated with 1 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs. For RBCs treated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs, the emission intensity was divided by 10.

Fluorescent properties of RBCs treated with the GQDs. Fluorescence emission spectra excited at 450 nm (a) and fluorescence excitation spectra monitored at 550 nm (b) for the suspensions of untreated RBCs (blue curves), RBCs incubated with 1 μg⋅mL-1 (red curves) or 50 μg⋅mL-1 (yellow curves) of the GQDs, and for the GQDs in PBS (divided by 60, dashed purple curves; the GQD concentration was 50 μg⋅mL-1). The spectra for the control sample were subtracted from the spectra for RBCs treated with the GQDs. The dotted green curve in panel (a) shows the absorption spectrum for the suspension of RBCs incubated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Bright-field and fluorescence images of RBCs acquired before and after irradiation. Before irradiation, RBCs were treated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs for 1 h. The light dose was 246 J⋅cm-2 at 470 nm. The circles mark the cells that are situated roughly perpendicular to the focal plane.

Bright-field images of RBCs incubated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs before, immediately after, and at 5 min after the light exposure with doses of 197 J⋅cm-2 and 246 J⋅cm-2. The irradiation was performed with a 470 nm LED built into the microscope.

Photodegradation of the GQDs. (a), (c)–(e) UV–Vis absorption spectra for 50 μg⋅mL-1 GQD suspensions in PBS acquired before and after exposure to laser radiation (450 nm, 52 mW). The measurements were performed for the GQD suspensions without additives (a) and with 1 mM of antioxidants: GSH (c), NAC (d), and H2Q (e). The exposure times are shown in the legends. (b) The absorbance at 488 nm vs. irradiation time for 50 μg⋅mL-1 GQD suspensions in PBS without or with additives: 9 mM of NaN3, 2 vol% of DMSO; 1 mM of GSH, NAC, and H2Q. (f) The absorbance at 488 nm vs. absorbed energy for 50 μg⋅mL-1 GQD suspensions in PBS without additives and with 1 mM of GSH, NAC, and H2Q.
The RBC viability as a function of the GQD concentration, light dose, and time elapsed after irradiation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ⁎ The expected systematic error of the measurements was estimated to vary between 3 and 5%.

head.ce:title: Photostability and phototoxicity of graphene quantum dots interacting with red blood cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nuclear Problems of the Belarusian State University, Bobruiskaya str. 11, Minsk 220006, Belarus

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Nuclear Problems of the Belarusian State University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nuclear Problems of the Belarusian State University, Bobruiskaya str. 11, Minsk, 220006, Belarus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: B.I. Stepanov Institute of Physics, NAS of Belarus, Nezavisimosty ave. 68, Minsk 220072, Belarus

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: B.I. Stepanov Institute of Physics, NAS of Belarus

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: B.I. Stepanov Institute of Physics, NAS of Belarus, Nezavisimosty ave. 68, Minsk, 220072, Belarus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we discuss fluorescent properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) interacting with the membranes of red blood cells. We report the results of spectroscopic, microscopic, and photon-counting measurements of the GQDs in different surroundings for uncovering specific features of the GQD fluorescence, and describe two observed phenomena important for implementation of the GQDs as fluorescent labels and agents for drug delivery. Firstly, the GQDs can suffer from photodegradation but also can be stabilized in the presence of antioxidants (reduced glutathione, N-acetylcysteine, or 1,4-hydroquinone). Secondly, GQDs can accumulate in red blood cell membranes without compromising the viability of the cells but also can induce hemolysis in the presence of visible light. We discuss mechanisms and regimes of the photodegradation, stabilization, interaction of the GQDs with red blood cell membranes, and hemolysis. Notably, photohemolysis for the case is dependent on the light dose and GQD concentration but not caused by the production of reactive oxygen species.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are biocompatible carbon-based nanomaterials with remarkable luminescent properties [1–3]. GQDs comply with the requirements for well-designed drug delivery systems. Firstly, GQDs exhibit biocompatibility [4–7] and intracellular accumulation [8–14]. The biosafety of GQDs was investigated in vitro on many cell lines [9,15–17] (including even primary hematopoietic stem cells [18]) and in vivo [8,11,19,20]. A general result of such research can be well summarized by a statement from a very recent review [21]: “These studies suggest that GQDs are nontoxic and biosafe materials”. Secondly, GQDs can be functionalized with various surface groups to achieve conjugation with drug molecules [4,5,8,22–24]. Thirdly, GQDs exhibit highly tunable photoluminescence [1,2,25,26] and are photostable [1,15,27,28], which permits their visualization during circulation and intracellular accumulation [4,5,29,30]. Fourthly, GQDs are biodegradable and can be eliminated from an organism by immune cells [29]. Finally, the versatility of GQDs allows their modification during the synthesis in such a way as to be selectively toxic, for example, by enabling pro- or antioxidant action [31,32]. The photoluminescent [2,25,26,33] and redox [22,31,32,34] activity of GQDs is affected by many factors including synthetic method, size, surface functional groups, and doping elements.

The mentioned characteristics make GQDs perspective candidates for a variety of applications termed now as “theranostics”, when the same active agent can be simultaneously used for diagnosing [4–6,16,29,30,35] and inducing therapeutic effects, for example, by drug delivery [25] or redox processes [36].

Light-induced generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by GQDs [7,11,17,37,38] as well as the conjugation of GQDs with photosensitizers [22,39] provides a synergistic combination of fluorescence imaging and photodynamic effect [17,22,40]. Ge et al. [11] showed high capability of GQDs to generate singlet oxygen with the yield exceeding 1.3. It was found that photodynamic processes of both type I (charge transfer leading to the formation of free radicals) and type II (energy transfer leading to the formation of singlet oxygen) are involved in the ROS generation by photoactivated GQDs [41]. In addition, GQDs exhibit redox activity and can act as electron donors and acceptors [22,31,32,34]. GQDs were shown to both quench and produce ROS in cell-free systems [32,34,36,42,43]. GQDs conjugated with riboflavin (RF) were demonstrated to accumulate in cancer cells and induce two-photon photodynamic effect [39].

GQDs usually enter cells via endocytosis [10,44] and are often distributed in the cytoplasm [8–14]. In difference with most mammalian cells, red blood cells (RBCs) cannot perform endocytosis [45]. GQDs can be expected to remain in the vicinity of the RBC membranes while interacting with them, and thus enable their visualization. The interactions of GQDs with RBCs were reported in several works. As shown in Ref. 46, the exposure of RBCs to graphene oxide induces hemolysis, while the incubation with GQDs only distorts cell membranes but preserves cell viability. According to Ref. 47, some kinds of GQDs can cause aberrant morphology of RBCs and hemolysis, whereas neither of these effects is observed for other kinds of GQDs. Kim et al. revealed that the dark toxicity of GQDs to RBCs depends on the GQD functionalization and concentration as well as exposure time [48]. It should be noted, however, that the effects of GQDs on RBCs were studied only in the absence of light. Also, the potential of GQDs as a fluorescent label for RBCs has not been examined.

In our previous works, we studied spectral properties of standardized and commercially available GQDs (CAS 7440-44-0, Sigma-Aldrich [49]) in different environments. Properties of such GQDs demonstrate great reproducibility from batch to batch, which is rarely attainable for GQDs synthesized in a lab. Optical and microscopic properties of these GQDs were already studied in our previous works [29,50] and by the manufacturer [49]. The GQDs of this kind were found to accumulate in neutrophils and trigger the production of hypochlorous acid leading to the degradation of the GQDs [29]. The aggregated GQDs on a substrate demonstrate a number of interesting emission properties, such as stochastic intermittent fluorescence and hysteresis [50]. These GQDs (aggregated on a substrate or dispersed in a polymer matrix) were also found to be quite unstable suffering significant photobleaching [50,51], in contrast to the data reported for other kinds of GQDs [1,15,27,28].

In this work, we examine heretofore unexploited possibilities of the bioimaging of RBCs with the help of GQDs. It was rather unexpected and counter-intuitive for us to find quite pronounced phototoxicity of these GQDs toward RBCs although the GQDs were not modified with the aim to be phototoxic. Also, this toxicity arises not from ROS generation. We study the interactions of the GQDs with RBCs under both dark and light conditions. To reveal the mechanisms of the phototoxicity and photobleaching of these GQDs, we investigate their photochemical transformations in aqueous suspensions. We study spectral properties and emission statistics of the GQDs incorporated into RBCs and assess their applicability as a fluorescent label.

Green luminescent, water-dispersed GQDs (CAS 7440-44-0, Sigma-Aldrich) were used in this work. As demonstrated in our previous work [50], this type of GQDs is characterized by a mean diameter of 17 nm and a mean thickness of 0.7 nm. The as-received aqueous suspension of the GQDs was diluted with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4, ionic strength 0.15 M). Riboflavin (RF), sodium azide (NaN3), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), reduced glutathione (GSH), N-acetylcysteine (NAC), 1,4-hydroquinone (H2Q), and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Human oxyhemoglobin (HbO2) was purified from freshly drawn blood by the method described elsewhere [52]. All substances except HbO2 were dissolved in PBS, and HbO2 solutions were prepared in Tris buffer (pH 7.4, ionic strength 0.15 M).

RBC samples were prepared from whole blood donated by healthy volunteers after a written consent. A 100 μL aliquot of fresh whole blood was diluted with 0.15 M NaCl solution so that the RBC concentration was 107 cells⋅mL-1. The resulting cell suspension was rinsed in NaCl solution with centrifugation (3000 rpm for 5 min) three times, and the cells were resuspended in PBS. The concentration of 107 cells⋅mL-1 was maintained at each step.

The RBCs were dark-incubated with 1 or 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs in PBS (pH 7.4) for 1 h. The cells were rinsed in PBS with centrifugation three times. These suspensions with 107 cells⋅mL-1 were used for spectroscopic measurements. For fluorescence microscopy, the RBC suspensions were diluted with PBS to 106 cells⋅mL-1 and transferred to cell culture imaging dishes with coverslip bottoms (Ibidi GmbH). The imaging dishes contained a rather large amount of the suspensions (3 mL) to avoid thermal effects. Microscopy measurements were performed after the sedimentation of RBCs in the imaging dishes for 15 min. Untreated RBCs in PBS were used as a control sample.

Cell viability was determined by counting the total amount of cells before irradiation and the amount of intact RBCs after irradiation using the NIS-Elements Ar imaging software. Each measurement of cell viability was repeated 3 to 5 times, and each sample contained at least 300 cells. The cytotoxicity of the GQDs toward RBCs, i.e., the hemolysis rate, was measured spectrophotometrically (a detailed description of this method can be found in the Supporting Information).

Cell imaging was carried out using an inverted Nikon Eclipse Ti2 microscope with a 40× (0.75NA) or 10× (0.3NA) Nikon CFI Plan Fluor DLL objective. The excitation light source was an LED peaking at 470 nm and with an FWHM of 27 nm. The LED was used for the fluorescence excitation as well as for the irradiation of the cells. The absolute irradiance of the LED was measured using Ophir 3 A-FS sensor. The fluorescence signal was separated from the excitation light using BrightLine Pinkel filter set (490–530 nm emission range) and detected using a monochrome DS-Qi2 camera with a 16 MP CMOS matrix.

Suspensions of RBCs treated with the GQDs were placed in a 1 mm quartz cuvette and irradiated at normal incidence with a 450 nm semiconductor laser diode. The fluorescence signal vs. time was recorded using a custom-made optical setup that was described in detail in our previous work [50]. The emission statistics were measured at the single-photon level using an ID Quantique ID100 detector (more details can be found in Ref. [51]).

To assess photostability of the GQDs in the presence of various additives, 50 μg⋅mL-1 suspensions of the GQDs in PBS were placed in a 1 cm quartz cuvette and subjected to 450 nm laser radiation. The optical power was 52 mW. The suspensions were constantly agitated with a magnetic stirring bar to ensure homogenous photochemical transformations. The extent of photodegradation was determined by measuring UV–Vis absorption spectra after irradiation.

The singlet oxygen luminescence was studied using a nanosecond laser NIR spectrometer developed at the B.I. Stepanov Institute of Physics of the National Academy of Sciences of Belarus [53,54]. Samples were excited by the second harmonic (532 nm) of a Nd:YAG laser (DTL-314QT, Laser-export). Typical parameters of the laser were as follows: the pulse width of 10 ns, the pulse energy of 1 μJ, and the repetition rate of 2.5 kHz. Luminescence radiation was collected with a high-throughput optical system, spectrally isolated using a bandpass filter with a transmittance peak at 1270 nm and a FWHM of 34 nm, and directed to a photomultiplier tube (H10330A-45, Hamamatsu Photonics) operated in the photon-counting mode. The measurements were performed for a 50 μg⋅mL-1 GQD suspension at room temperature (20±1 oC). The quantum yield was measured 5 times. To avoid the photodegradation of the sample during the measurement, the GQD suspension was constantly agitated using a magnetic stirring bar. The decrease in absorbance of the sample after the measurement did not exceed 3%.

The singlet oxygen quantum yield
γ Δ
was calculated using Eq. 1:
(1)
γ Δ s
=
γ Δ r
S s
S r
1 −
10
−
A r
1 −
10
−
A s
where S is the integrated intensity of the singlet oxygen luminescence and A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength. The sample and reference compound are denoted using symbols “s” and “r”, respectively. The reference compound was tetra-(N-methylpirydyl)-porphyrin (TMPyP4+) in aqueous solution with a known singlet oxygen quantum yield
γ Δ r
= 0.77 ± 0.04 [55].

All spectroscopic measurements were performed in 1 cm quartz cuvettes (Hellma). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Proscan MC122 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Horiba Scientific Fluorolog3® spectrofluorimeter. During the measurements, cell suspensions were constantly agitated using a magnetic stirring bar to minimize sedimentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Localization of the Graphene Quantum Dots in Red Blood Cells
ce:section.ce:para: First, we studied the effect of the GQDs on the morphology of RBCs. Fig. 1 shows bright-field microscopy images of RBCs incubated with 1 or 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs for 1 h. It can be seen that after being exposed to the GQDs, many of the RBCs turn into echinocytes, while the cells in the control sample retain their biconcave disc shape. Despite the modified morphology, no lysed RBCs are found even after incubating them with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs for 24 h (Table S1 in the Supporting Information). The GQD concentrations used in this work are lower by an order of magnitude compared to those applied in Ref. 48, where the hemolysis rate of RBCs was insignificant at GQD concentrations below 500 μg⋅mL-1.

The GQD accumulation in RBCs was visualized using fluorescent microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1, exposing RBCs to 1 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs for 1 h is sufficient for the nanomaterial to spread over the cell surface and to be registered by fluorescence microscopy under 470 nm excitation. RBCs incubated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs are characterized by bright fluorescence. The emission signal seems to be confined to cell membranes and is distributed quite uniformly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral Properties of the Graphene Quantum Dots in Red Blood Cells
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs studied in this work localize in the membranes of RBCs, exhibit bright fluorescence, and thus could be used for the imaging of RBCs. The UV–Vis absorption spectra for RBCs treated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs and for the control sample are almost identical (Fig. S1). The spectra are dominated by the absorption bands of HbO2 peaked at 415, 541, and 577 nm [52] and the light scattering on the cells. The bands of the GQDs cannot be discerned making absorbance not suitable for the detection of the GQDs in RBC suspensions.

Under 450 nm excitation, the RBC autofluorescence and the fluorescence of the GQDs are observed in the same spectral region (Fig. 2a). The emission spectrum for RBCs treated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs exhibits a wide band in the 500–700 nm range with a drop in intensity between 550 and 630 nm. The drop in fluorescence intensity coincides in position with the absorption bands of HbO2 and thus is due to the inner filter effect. The emission intensity is an order of magnitude higher compared to untreated RBCs. The emission intensity of RBCs incubated with 1 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs exceeds that of the control sample only 3-fold. The excitation spectra for RBCs incubated with the GQDs are similar to the spectrum for an aqueous GQD suspension (Fig. 2b).

Changes in spectral properties of the GQDs in RBC suspensions can occur due to reabsorption of the emitted signal by endogenous molecules (i.e., the inner filter effect) or due to energy transfer and fluorescence quenching. Hemoglobin is the primary light absorber in RBCs, and flavins are known to contribute to the autofluorescence of many cell types (including RBCs) in the 500–700 nm spectral range [56,57]. To assess the influence of endogenous molecules on the fluorescence of the GQDs in RBC suspensions, we studied the interactions of the GQDs with HbO2 and RF in aqueous solutions. The fluorescence of RF, one of the endogenous flavins, was previously shown to be quenched by graphene and graphene oxide [58]. The energy transfer from GQDs to RF was also demonstrated in Ref. 39.

As the fluorescence spectrum for the GQDs in RBC suspensions is deformed due to the presence of HbO2, we studied this effect in more detail in aqueous suspensions of the GQDs containing HbO2. At [GQDs] = 25 μg⋅mL-1 and [HbO2] = 7.5 μM, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum is dominated by the bands of HbO2, and the bands of the GQDs are considerably less intense. Both the excitation and emission spectra for the GQDs are deformed in the presence of HbO2 (Fig. S2). More specifically, the spectra become less intense, and the emission spectrum exhibits a slight drop in emission intensity similar to the spectrum for the GQDs in the RBC suspension (recall Fig. 2a). It was found that the observed changes are not associated with the quenching of the GQD luminescence by HbO2 but are rather due to the inner filter effect, i.e., the attenuation of the excitation beam by HbO2 and the reabsorption of the GQD luminescence by HbO2.

As follows from Fig. 1, the emission signal of the GQDs in RBCs is confined to cell membranes. As shown above, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs in RBC suspensions decreases as a result of the inner filter effect. However, due to a very small thickness of RBCs (∼2 μm [59]), the absorbance of RBCs in the 450–800 nm spectral region does not exceed 0.1. The light absorption by HbO2 is thus not responsible for the absence of the emission signal from the cytoplasm. This observation confirms that the GQDs accumulate in the RBC membranes and do not penetrate into the cells.

The interactions of GQDs with RF were also examined. To assess possible quenching effects, we acquired fluorescence excitation and emission spectra for aqueous suspensions with varying concentrations of the GQDs and a constant concentration of RF. Interestingly, RF and the studied type of GQDs are characterized by emission spectra that are almost identical in shape under 450 nm excitation (Fig. S3a).

Adding the fluorescent GQDs to an 8.8 μM RF solution does not seem to results in a proportional increase in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3b), which could be due to quenching. Since the emission spectra for RF and the GQDs are almost identical in shape, they are not suitable for extracting the contributions of the two emitters. We were able to assess the quenching effects by analyzing fluorescence excitation spectra (Fig. S4a, the method is described in detail in the Supporting Information). The obtained Stern-Volmer plot [60] is well fitted by a linear function (Fig. S4b) with the constant
K SV
≈ 0.02 L⋅mg-1. It can be seen that the GQDs indeed quench the fluorescence of RF. This
K SV
value should be considered a rather rough estimate since the emitter (RF) and the quencher (the GQDs) exhibit virtually identical emission spectra. For comparison, the quenching of the RF fluorescence in the presence of graphene oxide is characterized by
K SV
= 0.0022 ± 0.0001 L⋅mg-1 [58].

Thus, it was found that the fluorescence of the GQDs and RF almost coincide, and the GQDs can quench the fluorescence of RF. These facts must be taken into account when a small concentration of the GQDs is present in cells since the intracellular fluorescence of the GQDs can be misidentified as autofluorescence.

In spite of the strong light absorption by HbO2, light scattering on the cells, and autofluoresence background, the GQDs accumulate in RBCs, exhibit intense fluorescence in the visible range, and thus can be applied as a fluorescent label for RBCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light-Induced Effects in Red Blood Cells Treated with the Graphene Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: At the moment, the potential of GQDs as an agent for photodynamic therapy is intensely explored [11,40,61]. GQDs can be used as photosensitizers due to their ability to generate ROS [11,17,38,62–64]. In addition, this nanomaterial can combine functions of a drug delivery system and a fluorescent label [17,22,40].

As shown in Section 3.1, the GQDs studied in this work can cause morphology changes in RBCs without their lysis under dark conditions. The localization of the GQDs in cell membranes is plausible since RBCs cannot perform endocytosis [45]. Light-induced effects are expected to be more pronounced in RBCs compared to cells capable of endocytosis, where the GQDs are distributed over a much larger volume inside the cytoplasm [8–14]. RBCs loaded with the GQDs are thus a valuable model to investigate the photodynamic effect of this nanomaterial. The acquisition of the fluorescence microscopy images shown in Fig. 1 exposes RBCs to a light dose of ca. 1 J⋅cm-2. In this section, we also study the light-induced processes in RBCs treated with the GQDs that occur when the cells are exposed to substantially higher light doses.

As follows from Fig. 3 , the GQDs incorporated into RBCs continue to fluoresce after a light exposure of 246 J⋅cm-2 at 470 nm. However, in the bright-field images some of the cells turn pale and their membranes become smoother thus indicating their transformation into ghost cells (i.e., the membranes of RBCs that underwent lysis [65]). Due to weak adhesion of RBCs, some of the cells go out of focus during the bright-field imaging. However, the cells that are out of focus seem quite dark and blurred, while the ghost cells are pale but not blurred. The differences can be seen vividly in bright-field microscopy videos for untreated RBCs and RBCs treated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs (see Supplementary Material). Although RBC membranes lose integrity, no increase in fluorescence intensity is observed in the surrounding medium, confirming that the GQDs remain in the RBC membranes even after the lysis. The accumulation of the GQDs in RBC membranes can be seen particularly well in the cross section of the cells (marked with circles in Fig. 3).

The amount of lysed cells depends on the GQD concentration, light dose, and time elapsed after irradiation. These data are summarized in Table 1 and Fig. 4 . For RBCs incubated with 50 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs, hemolysis becomes evident at the light dose of ca. 200 J⋅cm-2 and increases for higher light doses. The percentage of lysed cells also increases with time after the end of irradiation. No hemolysis is observed for the control sample and for RBCs incubated with 1 μg⋅mL-1 of the GQDs even at the light dose of 740 J⋅cm-2 (Table 1). Light-induced hemolysis of RBCs treated with the GQDs was confirmed by spectrophotometrical registration of HbO2 in the extracellular medium (Section 5 and Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information).

One of the endogenous antioxidants, glutathione (GSH), can prevent oxidative damage in RBCs [66]. We attempted to prevent the lysis of RBCs by adding 3 mM of GSH to cell suspensions. The presence of the antioxidant slightly delayed the lysis of the cells, but no significant improvement in the overall cell viability was achieved (Table 1). GSH might be ineffective because it cannot access the GQDs incorporated into the RBC membranes. Another possible explanation is that the hemolysis occurs due to mechanisms of cell destruction that do not involve free radicals.

To the best of our knowledge, the light-induced lysis of RBCs incubated with GQDs is reported for the first time in this work. The hemolysis becomes significant as the light dose approaches 200 J⋅cm-2. This nanomaterial could still be used to visualize RBC membranes at sufficiently low light doses. Image acquisition in most wide-field microscopy techniques requires light doses that are several orders of magnitude lower than 200 J⋅cm-2. However, scanning laser microscopy can expose RBCs to high light doses thus causing the photodamage of the cells. A careful investigation of hemolysis under both dark and light conditions should be carried out before using any type of GQDs in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of the Graphene Quantum Dots and Photon-Counting Measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Many kinds of GQDs were reported to be highly photostable nanomaterials [1–3,15,27,28,67,68]. However, the GQDs studied in this work seem to lack photostability. Exposing the GQDs in RBC suspensions to 450 nm laser radiation leads to a fast decay of their fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6). This effect is also evident in the fluorescence microscopy images acquired before and after irradiating RBCs treated with the GQDs using the 470 nm LED built into the miscroscope (Fig. S7). Studying the emission statistics in the single-photon regime revealed that the photon-waiting times for the GQDs in RBC suspensions are well fitted with an exponential distribution (Fig. S8).

As shown in our previous works [50,51], the GQDs in other environments, namely, dispersed in various polymer matrices, immobilized in fixed and dried RBC membranes, or aggregated on a substrate, undergo similar photodegradation and demonstrate exponential distributions of the photon-waiting times. The instability of the GQDs is thus an intrinsic property of this nanomaterial and is barely affected by the microenvironment. The independence of the emission statistics on the aggregation state of the GQDs indicates that the dots emit independently. Consequently, potential aggregation of the GQDs inside the RBC membranes would not affect imaging experiments.

Both the light-induced lysis of RBCs treated with the GQDs and the photodegradation of the GQDs are possibly the manifestations of the same photochemical processes. Studying such processes is thus useful for understanding the underlying mechanism and, consequently, optimizing the use of this nanomaterial for biomedical applications.

Here, we investigate the photodegradation of the GQDs in aqueous suspensions. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum for the GQDs in an aqueous suspension contains absorption bands peaked at 275, 359, and 488 nm as well as a broad wing in the 550–650 nm range (Fig. 5a). The spectrum undergoes substantial changes as a result of the exposure to 450 nm laser radiation. The absorption band at 488 nm quickly decays under irradiation, while the other bands experience less pronounced changes. In the following, the absorbance at 488 nm is used to quantify the photodegradation of the GQDs.

Different kinds of GQDs can generate ROS during light exposure [11,17,37,38,62–64]. The generated ROS can potentially oxidize GQDs leading to changes in their spectral properties. We measured the singlet oxygen quantum yield for the GQDs studied in this work and found
γ Δ
= 0.015 ± 0.002 . Such a low quantum yield is unlikely to account for the fast photodegradation of the GQDs.

NaN3 and DMSO are known to be scavengers of singlet oxygen [69] and hydroxyl radical [70], respectively. Interestingly, the photodegradation of the GQDs is barely affected by the presence of these ROS scavengers (Fig. 5b). Hence, the photodegradation is unlikely to stem from the ROS production by the GQDs.

We attempted to inhibit the photodegradation of the GQDs by adding antioxidants (GSH, NAC, and H2Q). NAC contains thiol groups, like GSH [71], is biocompatible, and exhibits antioxidant properties [72]. H2Q is a widely used reducing agent [73].

In the presence of GSH, NAC, or H2Q, the decay of the absorbance at 488 nm is substantially retarded (Fig. 5c–e). The stabilization of this band is accompanied by the appearance of a shoulder in the 500–550 nm range. As can be seen from the inset of Fig. 5e, an intense band in the 240–260 nm range appears during the irradiation of the GQDs suspension containing H2Q. Since a similar band is present in the spectrum for BQ, we suggest that H2Q is oxidized to BQ by the GQDs in the presence of light. The dependence of the absorbance at 488 nm on the irradiation time for the GQD suspensions with and without antioxidants are compared in Fig. 5b. The highest photostability of the GQDs is achieved in the presence of H2Q, GSH is less efficient, and the least pronounced effect is caused by NAC.

When the absorbance at 488 nm is plotted vs. the energy absorbed by the GQD suspension rather than the irradiation time, almost linear trends are obtained (Fig. 5f). Fitting these data with linear functions allows us to quantify the effect of the antioxidants (the slopes of the fitting functions are shown in the legend of Fig. 5f). The rate of the photodegradation of the GQDs is reduced by an order of magnitude in the presence of 1 mM of H2Q. Adding 1 mM of GSH reduces the photodegradation rate 2-fold, and the effect of 1 mM of NAC is slightly weaker.

We also studied the photodegradation rates of the GQDs at different concentrations of added H2Q and GSH. Adding as little as 10 μM of H2Q already reduces the photodegradation rate 2-fold (Fig. S9a). At [H2Q] = 1 mM the GQDs are almost photostable. Complete stabilization of the GQDs with GSH is not achieved (Fig. S9b).

The observed inhibition of the GQD photodegradation in the presence of the antioxidants allows us to make assumptions about the underlying mechanism. The photodegradation is possibly caused by electron transfer processes from the GQDs to the environment and the resulting formation of free radicals on the surface of the GQDs. The added antioxidants may reduce the generated radicals and thus prevent subsequent chemical transformations. Based on the fact that ROS scavengers do not improve the photostability of the GQDs and in agreement with Ref. 36, we suggest that the formation of carbon-centered radicals at the surface of the photoexcited GQDs is responsible for the observed instability. The generated carbon-centered radicals may also be responsible for the lipid peroxidation in RBC membranes and, consequently, the lysis of the cells.

The GQDs studied in this work accumulate in the membranes of RBCs and cause changes in cell morphology without inducing hemolysis in the dark. The GQDs incorporated into RBC membranes exhibit bright, easily detectable fluorescence in the visible range, in spite of the presence of absorbing and fluorescing endogenous molecules (HbO2 and RF). During light exposure, the GQDs induce the lysis of RBCs. Depending on the light dose and GQD concentration, this nanomaterial can either visualize RBC membranes or induce hemolysis.

A rather fast degradation of the GQDs occurs during their exposure to visible light. The underlying photochemical processes possibly involve the formation of carbon-centered radicals at the surface of the GQDs. At the moment, we believe that such transformations of the GQDs incorporated into RBC membranes are responsible for both the light-induced hemolysis and the photoinstability of the GQDs.

The presented results have important implications for biomedicine. The GQDs would inevitably interact with RBCs and accumulate in their membranes after intravascular injection. Hemolysis induced by photoexcited GQDs is a dangerous side effect that should be taken into account before applying this nanomaterial in vivo. The affinity of the GQDs for cell membranes indicates that RBCs can be used as carriers for the GQDs and therapeutic agents bound to their surface. So, one can envision a personalized drug delivery system based on the vesicles derived from RBC membranes that are labeled with GQDs for visualization. However, for developing such a system, a careful investigation of the photostability and phototoxicity of the chosen kind of GQDs is needed.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Video 1: Microscopy video for RBCs untreated with the GQDs Supplementary Video 1
Supplementary Video 2: Microscopy video for RBCs treated with the GQDs Supplementary Video 2Microscopy video for RBCs treated with the GQDs
Supporting Information
Image 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2023.112800.

Tatsiana Kulahava: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Nikita Belko: Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Marina Parkhats: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Anastasiya Bahdanava: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization. Sergei Lepeshkevich: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Vyacheslav Chizhevsky: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Dmitri Mogilevtsev: Supervision, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

We thank our colleague Dr. Lena Golubewa for a fruitful discussion and valuable comments and the Center for Analytical and Spectral Measurements of the B.I. Stepanov Institute of Physics for fluorescence measurements.
